title: Relapse Prevention
subtitle: The most challenging part of recovery is not stopping use, it is preventing relapse over the long haul
author: Ned

seo.title: Relapse Prevention | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Relapse Prevention. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
It can be challenging to maintain sobriety in a world where many people use substances. Relapse prevention plans can help.

Detoxifying from substances can be physically grueling and often requires medical treatment. However, detox isn’t the hardest part of recovery. The hardest part is preventing relapse over the long haul and developing a large set of recovery skills.

One way to think about the project of relapse prevention is to think about the things that lead to relapse. There are two types of relapse contributors:

Overt contributors: the obvious triggers. You’re going to a wedding, and there’s an open bar. People around you are drinking heavily. You have two choices: avoid the wedding altogether, or develop a relapse prevention plan specific to this situation.

This plan could include:

  • always having a non intoxicating beverage
  • having an accountability partner
  • planning to stay only a short time
  • having a way to leave that doesn't rely on someone else
  • planning to see or talk to someone after the event

Before a person in recovery attends a wedding, they’ll want to be sure that they have all the possible risks accounted for. With high risk situations, "a failure to plan is a plan to fail."

Another example of an overt antecedent to relapse is a fight with a partner. Some persons with addiction have used substances or addictive behaviors to regulate their emotions after a fight (or to express their emotions during a fight).

Not allowing yourself adequate rest and recreation can cause you to be more likely to relapse.

So how do you create a relapse prevention plan for this scenario? Helpful actions can include:

  • learning to disengage effectively (i.e. collaboratively with your partner) when things start to heat up
  • having a friend to call for support while you vent and calm down
  • reminding yourself that anger is a very temporary emotion and then distracting yourself until you are in a more stable spot
  • and, of course, couples counseling can help partners learn to communicate (and even fight) in healthier ways

In addition to the overt antecedents to relapse, there are covert (or hidden) contributors. The most common covert antecedent to relapse is lifestyle imbalance. If you overwork or have too many demands in your life, with little relief or reward, you can become desperate for pleasure or relief.

Many people in recovery have found excercise to be helpful in preventing relapse. Marathon running is one that's particularly popular. Training for an intense physical event requires much time, may lead to a community of health-minded individuals, and provides a natural endorphin rush.

Eventually, such desperation will almost certainly overcome your self-control unless you find healthy ways to manage stress and relieve pressure. Developing a more balanced lifestyle often requires a person to confront core beliefs about their limitations and worth. It is impossible to take care of yourself unless you believe you require care and are worthy of it.

Untreated mental health problems such as depression, anxiety, and bipolar disorder also function as covert antecedents to relapse. Sustained recovery requires a certain degree of stability that is difficult to achieve in the face of untreated mental illness. Persons with co-occurring mental health problems should actively work to manage those problems with a psychiatrist and therapist who specialize in dual-diagnosis treatment.
